abstract: Practical activities are essential methodological tools in Science education, fostering critical thinking and student engagement with the natural world. Despite their importance, theoretical lectures often dominate the classroom. This study sought to evaluate, characterize, and promote the use of practical activities in two public schools in Vitória de Santo Antão, PE—one located in a rural area (Duque de Caxias) and the other in an urban setting (3 de Agosto). Data collection involved site visits and questionnaires administered to Science teachers. The findings indicated that the rural school (Duque de Caxias) possessed better infrastructure and demonstrated a higher teacher commitment to practical experimentation compared to the urban school. Educators from both institutions identified several barriers to implementing these activities, most notably the lack of physical resources and materials, overcrowded classrooms, and severe time constraints. To address these challenges and support the teaching-learning process, the researchers developed instructional workbooks covering content from the 6th to 9th grades of elementary school, providing educators and students with accessible strategies for integrating practical science in the classroom.
